ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for white bread with peanut butter; mapped to the closest parent class 'bread food product' (FOODON_00002473). A more specific composite-sandwich class does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for white bread with peanut butter as a composite dish. Mapped to the nearest parent category 'Bread and similar products' (A000E). Peanut butter component is not separately coded in this mapping.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for white bread with peanut butter as a composite food item.

serving.description: 2 slices white bread with peanut butter
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is two slices of white bread (approximately 60 g) spread with one to two tablespoons of peanut butter (approximately 20 g), totalling around 80 g.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with peanut butter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'White bread with peanut butter' (record ID F05020130). All per-100 g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 (data not available).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; White bread GI reference value
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: White bread with peanut butter as a composite dish does not have a single direct GI entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 71 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database reference value for white wheat bread (glucose reference = 100), which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this dish. Peanut butter has a very low GI (~14) and its fat and protein content moderates the overall glycaemic response somewhat; however, the white bread base dominates the carbohydrate load (approximately 70–75% of total carbohydrate in the composite). A blended GI estimate of 71 is used as a conservative upper bound consistent with the white bread base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (71) × carbohydrate per serving (43.12 g × 0.80 serving fraction = 34.5 g) / 100 = 24.5, rounded to 24.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 440 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; potassium at 109 mg/100 g is insufficient to offset this sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this food due to its high sodium content (440 mg/100 g); substituting low-sodium bread and natural no-added-salt peanut butter can substantially reduce the sodium contribution per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White bread base carries a high GI (~71) and delivers 43.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g; peanut butter's protein and fat partially moderate the glycaemic response but cannot fully offset the refined carbohydrate load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should replace white bread with a wholegrain or high-fibre bread to significantly lower the glycaemic index and glycaemic load, and choose natural peanut butter without added sugar to minimise postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 440 mg/100 g is a cardiovascular risk factor; total fat is 10.6 g/100 g with saturated fat breakdown not reported, and peanut butter contributes predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats which are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, choose natural peanut butter (high in MUFA/PUFA, no added salt) and low-sodium wholegrain bread to reduce sodium intake and improve the overall fatty acid profile of this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with peanut butter is a simple, widely consumed composite dish consisting of sliced white wheat bread spread with peanut butter. The white bread provides a soft, mild-flavoured base with a light, airy crumb, while the peanut butter contributes a rich, creamy or crunchy texture with a distinctly nutty, slightly sweet flavour. Per 100 g, the dish delivers 314 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 10.6 g fat, and 43.1 g carbohydrate, with 3.1 g dietary fibre and 440 mg sodium. The high sodium content (440 mg/100 g) makes this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension. The white bread base has a high glycaemic index (~71), making this combination a high-glycaemic-load meal that is not recommended for people with type 2 diabetes without modification. Peanut butter contributes heart-healthy monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats, which partially offset cardiovascular risk, but the elevated sodium remains a concern. The dish is a common breakfast and snack item in Singapore and internationally, often consumed as a quick, energy-dense meal. It is naturally dairy-free and suitable for vegetarians and vegans. Key allergens include gluten (from wheat bread) and peanuts. Substituting wholegrain bread and natural no-added-salt peanut butter significantly improves the nutritional profile for all three clinical conditions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F05020130), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The match is exact. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference value as the dominant carbohydrate contributor, which is the approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food item (F05020130) as of the accessed date. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source, consistent with the absence of animal-derived fat in standard peanut butter and the negligible cholesterol in white bread. The input GI value of 14 with 'low' classification was rejected as it is inconsistent with the well-established high GI of white bread (~71 per University of Sydney GI Database); the input GI source 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.3 is not an approved source. GI has been corrected to 71 (high) based on the University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference. The input health_suitability condition key 'diabetes' has been remapped to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Cardiovascular Disease condition added as required by Q7. dietary_tags 'high_fibre' from input is borderline (3.1 g/100 g meets the Singapore HPB threshold of ≥3 g/100 g for high-fibre claim on bread products) and is retained. 'plant_based' tag retained as the dish contains no animal products in its standard formulation.
